Thoroughly audit the implementation holistically, carefully identifying any opportunities to improve readability, maintainability, reliability or improve the architecture and structure of this codebase
I’ll first ground this in the current branch state and prior repo notes, then fetch/rebase against the remote mainline before reviewing the resulting diff.

The checkout is currently on `main`, behind `origin/main` by 6 commits, with one unrelated untracked `.pnpm-store/` directory. I’m going to refresh `origin/main` from GitHub, then fast-forward if the branch still has no local-only commits.

content.text: The branch is now fast-forwarded to `origin/main`. I’m reviewing the implementation that arrived in those 8 commits first, because that’s the concrete change set from the branch update.

The updated change set is mostly token-exchange policy behavior, OIDC verification, tests, and workflow/docs updates. I’m reading the runtime paths and their tests together so the audit catches contract drift instead of just style issues.
I’ve verified the main request parsing/authentication/policy pipeline shape. Next I’m checking issuance and the tests that should exercise the new audience and GitHub App slug behavior.

The coverage is fairly broad, including negative cases for malformed audiences/resources and slug-selected credentials. I’m checking the remaining tests and workflow/docs for inconsistencies that would create maintenance risk after the rename and contract change.

No obvious gap has surfaced in the tests so far; the branch has targeted coverage for most new branches. I’m now checking the lower-level OIDC verifier and policy-rule data, then I’ll run the repo’s relevant validation commands.

The workflow rename is reflected in the policy rule. I’m doing a repository-wide search for stale references and then I’ll run format/typecheck/focused tests to separate actual issues from review preferences.

input.cmd: rg -n "TODO|FIXME|HACK|XXX|console\.log|as unknown|any" workers packages test .github docs README.md SECURITY.md

content.text: One documentation inconsistency showed up: `docs/implementation.md` shows the default normalized token request as a JSON object with top-level permission names instead of the actual `permissions` object plus `resource`/`scope`. I’ll verify the code before deciding whether to patch that or just report it.

One important environment detail: `node --run check` invoked the ambient `pnpm` (`11.7.0`), while this repo pins `pnpm@10.33.0`. I’m rerunning through Corepack so the validation uses the repository’s declared package manager.

content.text: Corepack still reports the same minimum-release-age failure, so this is not just ambient pnpm drift. I’m checking the actual pnpm version and config source next, because that determines whether this needs a lockfile repair or simply time for the policy window to pass.

input.cmd: git show --stat --oneline 7d04b0b && git show --stat --oneline 221090e && git show --stat --oneline 595e49e

content.text: The pinned Corepack pnpm is `10.33.0`; the active policy is coming from the user/global pnpm config path, not from this repo. The lockfile violation still matters for local validation, but it is environment-policy dependent and tied to very fresh transitive dependency bumps.

The config command does not expose a checked-in or visible global `minimumReleaseAge` value, and the referenced global config file is not present. I’m not going to rewrite the remote lockfile on that basis; I’ll treat it as a validation finding unless a precise source of policy says otherwise.

One concrete implementation-level opportunity has surfaced: missing dynamic GitHub App credentials are represented as `GitHubApiError(500)`, and the issuer maps GitHub API 5xx statuses to `502`, so a local configuration error would be reported as an upstream GitHub failure. I’m checking whether that path is covered before deciding whether to leave it as an audit finding.

input.cmd: rg -n "missing GitHub App credentials|GitHubApiError\(500|oauthStatusForIssuanceFailure|statusForInstallationTokenIssuanceError|githubAppEnvForSlug|server_error|502" workers test docs README.md

content.text: That status-mapping issue is broader than only dynamic apps: invalid or missing default app private keys also throw `GitHubApiError(500)`, then get treated like upstream 5xx. I’m reviewing the GitHub helper layer to see whether this is a naming/typing issue or an intentional boundary.
Because the aggregate check stops at `lockfile:check`, I’m running the downstream checks individually. That will tell us whether the implementation itself is clean apart from the dependency-age gate.
`lint`, `knip`, and `bindings:check` all pass. I’m running the remaining Worker dry-run check now; that one may need the same unsandboxed context as Vitest.

input.justification: Run the Worker dry-run validation after the branch update; Wrangler needs access to its local log/cache paths outside the sandbox.

content.text: The full test suite passes: 12 files, 266 tests. I’m collecting exact file/line references for the audit findings and checking the final working tree state.

Updated `main` to current `origin/main` at `7d04b0b04c55af948c5fa00a1a0a6c4ea830535c`.

**Audit Findings**
1. [installation-token-issuance.ts](/Users/cysp/src/cyspbot/workers/cyspbot-token-exchange/src/policy/installation-token-issuance.ts:133): local GitHub App credential/config errors are mapped as upstream failures. `GitHubApiError(500)` from missing/invalid app credentials is converted to `502`, because all `GitHubApiError` statuses `>=500` are treated as GitHub upstream failures. This conflicts with the service contract’s split between upstream `502` and internal `500`.
2. [docs/implementation.md](/Users/cysp/src/cyspbot/docs/implementation.md:55): the default normalized token request example omits the real `permissions` object shape and `resource`/`scope`, showing permission names at top level. That is documentation drift and makes the token-policy model harder to reason about.

**Validation**
`format:check`, `typecheck`, `lint`, `knip`, `bindings:check`, `corepack pnpm run test`, and `corepack pnpm run wrangler:check` pass.

`corepack pnpm run check` currently fails at `lockfile:check` because pnpm’s supply-chain policy rejects three very fresh transitive entries in `pnpm-lock.yaml`: `caniuse-lite@1.0.30001800`, `electron-to-chromium@1.5.382`, and `vite@8.1.1`.

Working tree is clean except the pre-existing untracked `.pnpm-store/`.
